questions.q: What field in the pod spec do you set to bypass the kube-scheduler and manually assign a pod to a node?
questions.explanation: The nodeName field in the pod spec, when set at creation time, causes the kube-scheduler to skip the pod and the kubelet on that node picks it up directly.

questions.q: You need to change the node a running pod is assigned to. The pod was created without a nodeName. What is the correct approach?
options: kubectl edit pod and change nodeName
kubectl patch pod with the new nodeName
kubectl replace --force -f pod.yaml with updated nodeName
kubectl taint the current node to evict the pod
questions.explanation: nodeName is an immutable field after creation. kubectl replace --force deletes and recreates the pod from the updated spec, allowing the new nodeName to take effect.

questions.q: Which API resource/endpoint can you POST to in order to assign an already-pending pod to a specific node when the scheduler is unavailable?
questions.explanation: The Binding API (POST /api/v1/namespaces/{ns}/pods/{pod}/binding) allows you to manually assign a pending pod to a node, mimicking what the scheduler does.

questions.q: You can change the nodeName of a running pod using `kubectl edit pod`.
questions.explanation: False. nodeName is immutable after pod creation. kubectl edit will reject the change. You must use kubectl replace --force -f with the updated spec.
